that in the 1980's techniques will be available to detect second-order effects of general relativity. At that point the ratio of theoretical to experimental relativists may take a sharp turn downwards.”

Actually, there are a few exceptions to the claim that the post-Newtonian approximation suffices for the $1 9 7 0 ^ { \circ } { \mathsf { s } } .$ These exceptions occur where the external universe impinges on and influences the solar system. For example, gravitational waves propagating into the solar system from distant sources (Chapters 35-37) are ignored by every post-Newtonian approximation and by the PPN framework. They must be treated using a full metric theory or a weak-field,“fast-motion” approximation to such a theory. Similarly, time-dependence of the“gravitational constant"(840.8), induced in some theories by expansion of the universe, is beyond the scope of the PPN formalism, as is the expansion itself..

The PPN formalism is used not only in interpreting experimental tests of gravitation theories, but also as a powerful tool in theoretical astrophysics. By specializing all the PPN parameters to unity, except $\zeta = \eta = 0$ , one obtains the post-Newtonian approximation to Einstein's theory of gravity. This post-Newtonian approximation can then be used (and has been used extensively) to calculate general relativistic corrections to such phenomena as the structure and stability of stars.\*

Applications of PPN formalism to astrophysics

# Historical and Notational Notes

The earliest parametrizations of the post-Newtonian approximation were performed, and used in interpreting solar system experiments, by Eddington (l922), Robertson (1962),and Schiff (l962,l967). However,they dealt solely with the vacuum gravitational feld outside an isolated, spherical body (the sun). Nordtvedt (l968b,1969) devised the first full PPN formalism, capable of treating all aspects of the solar system; he treated the sun, planets,and moon as made from“gases" of point-particles (atoms) that interact gravitationally and clectromagnetically.Will(l97lc) later used techniques devised by Chandrasekhar (l965a) to modify Nordtvedt's formalism, so that it employs a stressed, continuous-matter description of celestial bodies. The version of the formalism presented here,_devised_by Will and Nordtvedt (1972), generalizes all previous versions to acquire“post-Galilean invariance"[see Chandrasekhar and Contopolous (l967)]. The most detailed and up-to-date review article on the PPN formalism is Will (1972).

In the literature of post-Newtonian physics and the PPN formalism, the Newtonian potential is described traditionally not by $\pmb { \phi }$ but by

# History and notation of PPN formalism

$$
U \equiv - \phi \equiv + \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } ) d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } } { \vert x - x ^ { \prime } \vert } .
$$

To avoid confusion, this chapter and the next will use $U .$ although the rest of the book uses $\pmb { \phi }$ ，

Turn now to a detailed, Track-2 exposition of the PPN formalism.

# $\$ 39.4$ .PPN COORDINATE SYSTEM

The PPN formalism covers the solar system (or whatever system is being analyzed) with coordinates $( t , x _ { j } ) \equiv ( t , x ^ { j } )$ that are as nearly globally Lorentz as possible:

$$
g _ { \alpha \beta } = \eta _ { \alpha \beta } + h _ { \alpha \beta } , ~ | h _ { \alpha \beta } | \lesssim M _ { \odot } / R _ { \odot } \sim 1 0 ^ { - 6 } .
$$

![](../chunks/Gravitation (Misner, Charles W, Thorne, Kip S. etc.) (z-library.sk, 1lib.sk, z-lib.sk)_part_0016_pages_1051-1120/auto/images/8d8f06e29c929a23e0ab3ee78815caf448b8030435b7729b37d6ec363bdc1621.jpg)

# EXPOSITION OF PPN FORMALISM:

Coordinate system

The rest of this chapter is   
Track 2. No earlier Track-2   
material is needed as   
preparation for it,but the   
followingwill behelpful:   
(1）Chapter7 (incompatibility of gravity and special relativity)   
12 $\$ 123$ (no prior geometry);   
(3)6636.9-36.11 (generation of gravitational waves); and   
(4）Chapter 38 (tests of foundations).

This chapter is not needed as preparation for any later chapter,but it will be helpful in Chapter 40 (solar-system tests)

(In this sense the PPN formalism is like linearized theory; see Chapter 18.) The velocity of the coordinate system (i.e., 4-velocity of its spatial origin) is so chosen that the solar system is approximately at rest in these coordinates. (Whether the center of mass of the solar system is precisely at rest, or is moving with some low velocity $v \lesssim ( M _ { \odot } / R _ { \odot } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sim 1 0 ^ { - 3 } \sim 3 0 0 ~ \mathrm { k m / s e c }$ ，is a matter for the user of the formalism to decide. For more on the options, see Ss39.9 and 39.12.)

The PPN coordinates provide one with a natural $^ { 6 6 } 3 + 1 ^ { , , }$ split of spacetime into space plus time. That split is conveniently treated using the notation of three-dimen-sional, flat-space vector analysis-even though spacetime and the three-dimensional hypersurfaces $x ^ { 0 } =$ constant are both curved. The resultant three-dimensional formalism willook more like Newtonian theory than like general relativity-as, indeed, one wishes it to;after all, one's goal is to study small relativistic corrections to Newtonian theory!

# \$39.5. DESCRIPTION OF THE MATTER IN THE SOLAR SYSTEM

Relative to the PPN coordinates, the matter of the solar system (idealized as a stressed medium) has a coordinate-velocity field

$$
v _ { j } \equiv d x _ { j } / d x ^ { 0 } .
$$

Choose an event ${ \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ ，and in its neighborhood transform to an orthonormal frame that moves with the matter there. Orient the spatial axes $\pmb { e } _ { \hat { j } }$ of this comoving frame So that they coincide as accurately as possible with the PPN coordinate axes. (This requirement will be made more precise in $\ S 3 9 . 1 0 .$ ）In the orthonormal comoving frame,define the following quantities, which describe the state of the matter:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( \mathrm { p r e s s u r e } ) \equiv p \equiv \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( t _ { \hat { x } \hat { x } } + t _ { \hat { y } \hat { y } } + t _ { \hat { z } \hat { z } } ) } \\ & { \qquad \equiv ( \mathrm { a v e r a g e ~ o f ~ s t r e s s ~ o v e r ~ a l l ~ d i r e c t i o n s } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Anisotropies (i.e., shears) in the stress are important only in planets such as the Earth;and even there they are dominated by the isotropic pressure:

$$
t _ { \hat { \imath } \hat { \jmath } } = p \delta _ { i j } + p \times [ \mathrm { c o r r e c t i o n s } \ll 1 ] .
$$

For many purposes,especially inside the sun,one can ignore the anisotropies, thereby approximating the solar-system matter as a perfect fuid.\*

The isotropic part of the radiation field gives a significant contribution to the pressure, ${ \pmb P }$ ,and the density of internal energy, $\pmb { \rho _ { o } } \varPi$ ,inside the sun. However, the anisotropic radiation flux is ignored in the stress-energy tensor. This approximation is allowable because in the sun the outward energy flux carried by radiation is less than $1 0 ^ { - 1 5 }$ of the internal energy density $\pmb { \rho _ { o } } \varPi$ ； in planets it is even less.

# $\$ 39.6$ .NATURE OF THE POST-NEWTONIAN EXPANSION

For any gravitationally bound configuration such as the solar system, the Newtonian approximation imposes limits on the sizes of various dimensionless physical quantities (see exercise 39.1):

Relative magnitudes of expansion parameters

(The Newtonian potential at the center of the sun is $\epsilon ^ { 2 } \sim 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ . The values of $p / \pmb { \rho _ { o } }$ $t _ { i j } / \rho _ { o }$ ,and $\pi$ there are also ${ \sim } 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ ,and they are much smaller elsewhere. The orbital velocities of the planets are all less than $1 0 0 \mathrm { k m } / \mathrm { s e c } = 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ ，so $v ^ { 2 } < 1 0 ^ { - 7 } . ,$ Moreover, changes with time of all quantities at fixed $x _ { j }$ are due primarily to the motion of the matter. As a result, time derivatives are small by $O ( \epsilon )$ compared to space derivatives,

$$
\Bigl \{ \frac { \partial A / \partial t } { \partial A / \partial x _ { j } } \Bigr | \sim | v _ { j } | \lesssim \epsilon \ \mathrm { f o r \ a n y \ q u a n t i t y } \ A ,
$$

although not in the radiation zone, where outgoing gravitational waves flow (distance $\stackrel { \textstyle > } { \sim }$ one light year from Sun). Consequently, the radiation zone must be excluded from the analysis when one makes a post-Newtonian expansion. To treat it requires different techniques, e.g.,those of Chapter 36.

Conditions 39.7 and 39.8 suggest that one expand the metric coefficients in powers of the small parameter e, treating $U , v ^ { 2 } , p / \rho _ { o } , t _ { i j } / \rho _ { o } ,$ and $\pi$ as though they were all of $O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ (often they are smaller!),and treating time derivatives as $O ( \epsilon )$ smaller than space derivatives.

In this “post-Newtonian” expansion, terms odd in $\boldsymbol { \epsilon }$ (i.e., terms such as

Rules of the expansion

$$
\int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) v _ { j } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } \sim \frac { M } { R } v \sim \epsilon ^ { 3 }
$$

whose total number of u's and $( \hat { d } / \hat { d } t ) ^ { \ast }$ is odd） change sign under time reversal, whereas terms even in e do not. Time reversal $( x ^ { \mathfrak { J } } = - x ^ { 0 } )$ also changes the sign of $g _ { 0 j } ( g _ { \overline { { { 0 } } } j } = - g _ { 0 j } )$ , but leaves $g _ { 0 0 }$ and $g _ { j k }$ unchanged. Therefore, $g _ { 0 j }$ must contain only terms odd in e; whereas $g _ { 0 0 }$ and $g _ { j k }$ must contain only even terms. (Actually, this ceases to be the case when radiation damping enters the picture.In the real world-one always insists on outgoing-wave boundary conditions. But time reversal converts outgoing waves to ingoing waves; so an extra sign change is required to convert back to out. Therefore,radiation damping terms in the near-zone metric are even in e for $g _ { 0 j }$ but odd for $g _ { 0 0 }$ and $g _ { j k }$ .However,radiation damping does not come into play until order $\epsilon ^ { 5 }$ beyond Newtonian theory-see Chapter 36-so it will be ignored here.)

The form of the expansion is already known through Newtonian order (see $\ S 1 7 . 4$ ， with $\pmb { \phi }$ replaced by $- U )$ : Newtonian gravity is only obtained when one demands that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { g _ { 0 0 } = - 1 + 2 U + \mathrm { [ t e r m s \leq \epsilon ^ { 4 } ] } , } \\ & { g _ { 0 j } = \mathrm { [ t e r m s \leq \epsilon ^ { 3 } ] } , } \\ & { g _ { i j } = \delta _ { i j } + \mathrm { [ t e r m s \leq \epsilon ^ { 2 } ] } . } \end{array}
$$

The stated limits on the higher-order corrections are dictated by demanding that the space components of the geodesic equation agree with the Newtonian equation of motion:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { j } } { d t ^ { 2 } } { \approx } \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { j } } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } = - { r ^ { j } } _ { \alpha \beta } \frac { d x ^ { \alpha } } { d \tau } \frac { d x ^ { \beta } } { d \tau } { \approx } - { r ^ { j } } _ { \alpha \beta } \frac { d x ^ { \alpha } } { d t } \frac { d x ^ { \beta } } { d t } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad = - { r ^ { j } } _ { 0 0 } - 2 { r ^ { j } } _ { 0 k } v _ { k } - { r ^ { j } } _ { k i } v _ { k } v _ { i } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad = U _ { j } + \mathrm { t e r m s ~ o f ~ o r d e r ~ } \{ \epsilon g _ { 0 k , j } ; \epsilon ^ { 2 } g _ { k l , j } \} . } \end{array}
$$

One would get the wrong Newtonian limit f $g _ { 0 k }$ were $O ( \epsilon )$ or greater, and if $g _ { k \ell } - \delta _ { k \ell }$ were $O ( 1 )$ or greater.

The above pattern continues on to al orders in the expansion. Thus in the geodesic eqtio $T ^ { \alpha \beta } { } _ { ; \beta } = 0$ $g _ { 0 0 }$ always goes hand-in-hand with ${ \epsilon } _ { { \delta } \mathfrak { a } }$ and $\epsilon ^ { 2 } g _ { j k }$ (see exercise 39.2). Therefore, the post-Newtonian expansion has the form summarized in Box 39.3.

# \$39.7. NEWTONIAN APPROXIMATION

At Newtonian order the metric has the form (39.10); and the 4-velocity and stress-Newtonian approximatio energy tensor have components, relative to the PPN coordinate system,

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { u ^ { 0 } = + 1 + O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) , } } & { { u ^ { j } = v _ { j } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) ; } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { T ^ { 0 0 } = \rho _ { o } + O ( \rho _ { o } \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) , } } & { { T ^ { 0 j } = \rho _ { o } v _ { j } + O ( \rho _ { o } \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { T ^ { j k } = t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } + \rho _ { o } v _ { j } v _ { k } + O ( \rho _ { o } \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

(see exercise 39.3). Two sets of equations govern the structure and evolution of the solar system.(l) The Einstein field equations.As was shown in $\ S 1 8 . 4$ , and also in $\ S 1 7 . 4$ , in the Newtonian limit Einstein's equations reduce to Laplace's equation

$$
U _ { , j j } = - 4 \pi \rho _ { o } ,
$$

which has the “action-at-a-distance” solution

$$
\dotsb \dotsb U ( \pmb { x } , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | \pmb { x } - \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } .
$$

Box 39.3 POST-NEWTONIAN EXPANSION OF THE METRIC COEFFICIENTS

(2) The law of local energy-momentum conservation, $T ^ { \alpha \beta } { } _ { ; \beta } = 0$ . The time component of this law reduces to the conservation of rest mass

$$
\partial \rho _ { o } / \partial t + \hat { c } ( \rho _ { o } v _ { j } ) / \partial x _ { j } = 0 + \mathrm { f r a c t i o n a l ~ e r r o r s ~ o f } \ O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) ;
$$

and the space components reduce to Newton's second law of motion, $^ { * } F = m a ^ { * }$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho _ { o } d v _ { j } / d t = \rho _ { o } ( \partial U / \partial x _ { j } ) - \partial t _ { j k } / \partial x _ { k } + \mathrm { f r a c t i o n a l ~ e r r o r s ~ o f } \ O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ { d / d t \equiv ( \mathrm { t i m e ~ d e r i v a t i v e ~ f o l l o w i n g ~ t h e ~ m a t t e r } ) \equiv \partial / \partial t + v _ { k } \partial / \partial x _ { k } } \end{array}
$$

(see exercise 39.3).

Equations (39.14)-(39.16),together with equations of state describing the planetary and solar matter,are the foundations for all Newtonian calculations of the structure and motion of the sun and planets. Notice that the internal energy density $\pmb { \rho _ { o } } \varPi$ nowhere enters into these equations. It is of no importance to Newtonian hydrodynamics. It maters for the sun's thermal-energy balance; but that is irrelevant here.

# Exercise 39.4. A USEFUL FORMULA

Derive from equations (39.15) the following useful formula,valid for any function $f ( { \pmb x } , t )$

$$

$$

Here both integrals are extended over all of space; and $d f / d t$ is the derivative following the matter (39.16).

$$

# Exercise 39.6. NEWTONIAN VIRIAL THEOREMS

(a) From equation (39.19'） show that

$$
d ^ { 2 } I _ { j k } / d t ^ { 2 } = 2 \int { ( \mathrm { t } } _ { j k } + t _ { \uparrow \hat { k } } + \rho _ { o } v _ { j } v _ { k } ) d ^ { 3 } x + \mathrm { f r a c t i o n a l ~ e r r o r s ~ o f } \ O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

where $I _ { j k }$ is the second moment of the system's mass distribution,

$$
I _ { j k } = \int \rho _ { o } x _ { j } x _ { k } d ^ { 3 } x .
$$

This iscalled the“time-dependent tensor virial theorem.”

(b) From this infer that, if<Ylongtime denotes an average over a long period of time, then

$$
\biggl \langle \int ( \mathrm { t } _ { j k } + t _ { \bar { j } \bar { k } } + \rho _ { o } v _ { j } v _ { k } ) d ^ { 3 } x \biggr \rangle _ { \mathrm { l o n g t i m e } } = { \cal O } \left( \int \rho _ { o } \epsilon ^ { 4 } d ^ { 3 } x \right) .
$$

This is called the“tensor virial theorem.”

(c) By contraction of indices and use of equations (39.18),(39.l4a),and (39.5e),derive the (ordinary) virial theorems:

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } d ^ { 2 } I / d t ^ { 2 } = \int \rho _ { o } \nu ^ { 2 } d ^ { 3 } x - \int \frac { 1 } { 2 } \rho _ { o } U d ^ { 3 } x + 3 \int p d ^ { 3 } x + \mathrm { f r a c t i o n a l ~ e r r o r s ~ o f } \ O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { I }$ is the trace of the second moment of the mass distribution

$$
I = I _ { j j } = \int { \rho _ { o } r ^ { 2 } d ^ { 3 } x } ;
$$

and

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \underbrace { \left. \int \rho _ { 0 } \nu ^ { 2 } d ^ { 3 } x \ - \ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \int } \rho _ { 0 } U d ^ { 3 } x \ + \ \underbrace { 3 \int p d ^ { 3 } x } _ { \mathrm { 1 0 n g ~ t i m e } } \right. _ { \mathrm { l o n g ~ t i m e } } } = O { \Big ( } \int \rho _ { 0 } \epsilon ^ { 4 } d ^ { 3 } x { \Big ) } . } \\ & { 2 \times { \Big ( } { \mathrm { e n e t i c } } { \Big ) } + { \Big ( } { \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l } } { \Big ) } + 3 \times { \Big ( } { \mathrm { p r e s s u r e } } { \Big ) } } \end{array} }
$$

# ExerCise 39.7. PULSATION FREQUENCY FOR NEWTONIAN STAR

Use the ordinary, time-dependent virial theorem (39.2la) to derive the following equation for the fundamental anguiar frequency of pulsation of a nonrotating, Newtonian star:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \omega ^ { 2 } = ( 3 \bar { \cal Y } _ { 1 } - 4 ) \frac { \left| \mathrm { s t a r } ^ { * } s \mathrm { ~ s e l f . } \underline { { \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ e n e r g y } } } \right| } { \left( \mathrm { t r a c e ~ o f ~ s e c o n d ~ m o m e n t ~ o f ~ s t a r } ^ { * } s \mathrm { ~ m a s s ~ d i s t r i b u t i o n } \right) } ; } \\ & { \qquad \bar { \cal T } _ { 1 } = \left( \begin{array} { l } { \mathrm { p r e s s u r e - w e i g h t e d ~ a v e r a g e } } \\ { \mathrm { o f ~ a d i a b a t i c ~ i n d e x } } \end{array} \right) \equiv \frac { \int { \cal Y } _ { 1 } p d ^ { 3 } x } { \int p d ^ { 3 } x } . } \end{array}
$$

In the derivation assume that the pulsations are “homologous”-ie., that a fuid element with equilibrium position $x ^ { j }$ (relative to center of mass $x ^ { j } = 0$ gets displaced to $x ^ { j } + \xi ^ { j } ( x , t ) ,$ where

$$
\xi ^ { j } = ( \mathrm { s m a l l ~ c o n s t a n t } ) x ^ { j } e ^ { - \mathrm { i } \omega t } .
$$

Assume nothing else. Notes: (l） The result (39.22） was derived differently in Box 26.2 and used in $\ S 2 4 . 4 $ (2）The assumption of homologous pulsation is fully justified if $\{ { \cal { T } } _ { 1 } - 4 / 3 \} =$ constant $\ll 1$ : see Box 26.2.(3) The result (39.22) is readily generalized to slowly rotating Newtonian stars: see,e.g.，Chandrasekhar and Lebovitz (l968). It can also be generalized to nonrotating post-Newtonian stars using general relativity $( { \tt B o x } 2 6 . 2 )$ .or using the PPN formalism for any metric theory [Ni (1973)]. And it can be generalized to slowly rotating,Post-Newtonian stars [see,e.g., Chandrasekhar and Lebovitz(1968)].

# \$39.8. PPN METRIC COEFFICIENTS

# Post-Newtonian corrections to metric:

(1）rules governing forms

The post-Newtonian corrections $k _ { \alpha \beta }$ to the metric coefficients $g _ { \alpha \beta }$ are calculated, in any metric theory of gravity,by lengthy manipulations of the field equations. (See,e.g.,exercise 39.l4 near the end of this chapter for general relativity.） But without ever picking some one theory,and without ever writing down any set of field equations,one can infer the forms of the post-Newtonian corrections $k _ { \alpha \beta }$ . Their forms are fixed by the following constraints: (l） They must be of post-Newtonian order $( k _ { 0 0 } \sim \epsilon ^ { 4 } , k _ { 0 j } \sim \epsilon ^ { 3 } , k _ { i j } \sim \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ .(2)They must be dimensionless. (3) $k _ { 0 0 }$ must be a scalar under rotations, $k _ { 0 j }$ must be components of a 3-vector, and $k _ { j k }$ must be components of a 3-tensor. (4) The corrections must die out at least as fast as $1 / r$ far from the solar system, so that the coordinates become globally Lorentz and spacetime becomes flat at $r = \infty$ .(5) For simplicity, one can assume that the metric components are generated only by $\rho _ { o } , \rho _ { o } \varPi , t _ { i \hat { j } } , p .$ ，products of these with the velocity $v _ { j } ,$ and time-derivatives of such quantities;\* but not by their spatial gradients.[This assumption of simplicity is satisfied by all metric theories examined up to 1973, except Whitehead (1922)and theories reviewed by Will(l973)-which disagree with experiment.] Note the further justification for this assumption in exercise 39.8.

(2) construction of corrections

Begin with the corrections to the spatial components, $k _ { i j } \sim \epsilon ^ { 2 }$ .There are only two functionals of $\rho _ { o } , p , \varPi , t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } , v _ { \dot { j } } ;$ ,that die out at least as fast as $1 / r$ ,are dimensionless, are $O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ , and are second-rank,symmetric 3-tensors; they are

$$
\delta _ { i j } U ( x , t ) ; \quad U _ { i j } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ( x _ { i } - x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } .
$$

Thus, $k _ { i j }$ must be $k _ { i j } = 2 \gamma \ : \delta _ { i j } U + 2 { \varGamma } U _ { i j } ,$ for some constant “PPN parameters" $\gamma$ and $r$ By an infinitesimal coordinate transformation $[ x _ { i \mathrm { N E W } } = x _ { i \mathrm { O L D } } + T \partial \chi / \partial x _ { i }$ ,with $\begin{array} { r } { \boldsymbol { x } ( \boldsymbol { x } , t ) = - \int \rho _ { o } ( \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) \vert \boldsymbol { x } - \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime } \vert d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ] } \end{array}$ one can set $T = 0$ ,thereby obtaining

$$
g _ { i j } = \delta _ { i j } + k _ { i j } = \delta _ { i j } ( 1 + 2 \gamma U ) + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) .
$$

Next consider $k _ { 0 j } \sim \epsilon ^ { 3 }$ .Trial and error yield only two vector functionals that die out as $1 / r$ or faster, are dimensionless and are $O ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } )$ : They are

$$
V _ { j } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) v _ { j } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
W _ { j } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot v ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ] ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } .
$$

Thus, $k _ { 0 j }$ must be a linear combination of these, involving unknown constants (PPN parameters) $\pmb { \mathscr { \Delta } } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { \Delta } } _ { 2 }$ ：

$$
g _ { 0 j } = k _ { 0 j } = - \frac { 7 } { 2 } { \cal { \cal { A } } } _ { 1 } V _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \cal { \cal { A } } } _ { 2 } W _ { j } + { \cal { O } } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) .
$$

Finally consider $k _ { 0 0 } \sim \epsilon ^ { 4 }$ Trial and error yields a variety of terms, which can all be combined together with the Newtonian part of $g _ { 0 0 }$ to give

$$
g _ { 0 0 } = - 1 + 2 U + k _ { 0 0 } = - 1 + 2 U - 2 \beta U ^ { 2 } + 4 \Psi - \xi \mathcal { Q } - \eta \mathcal { P } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \Psi ( \dot { \bar { x } } , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) \psi ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \psi = \beta _ { 1 } v ^ { 2 } + \beta _ { 2 } U + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta _ { 3 } { \cal T } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \beta _ { 4 } p / \rho _ { o } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \displaystyle \mathcal { U } ( \dot { x } , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot v ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ] ^ { 2 } } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\phi ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \bigg [ t _ { j k } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \delta _ { j k } t _ { l l } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) \bigg ] ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) ( x _ { k } - x _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } .
$$

Also, $\beta , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 3 } , \beta _ { 4 } , \xi , \eta$ are unknown constants (PPN parameters). Elsewhere in the literature the term $- \eta \mathcal { D }$ in $g _ { 0 0 }$ is ignored (see footnote on p.1075).

Yet another term is possible: one could have set

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { g _ { 0 0 } = [ \mathrm { v a l u e ~ i n ~ e q u a t i o n ~ } ( 3 9 . 2 3 \mathrm { f } ) ] } } \\ { { \phantom { g _ { 0 0 } = [ \mathrm { v a l u e ~ i n ~ e q u a t i o n ~ } ( 3 9 . 2 3 \mathrm { f } ) ] } - \Sigma \int \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot ( x ^ { \prime } - x ^ { \prime \prime } ) ] } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | | x ^ { \prime } - x ^ { \prime \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \Sigma }$ is another PPN parameter.[It can be shown, using the Newtonian equations (39.14)-(39.16),that this expression dies out as $1 / r$ far from the solar system.] If

such a $\pmb { \Sigma }$ term had been included, then one could have removed it by making the infinitesimal coordinate transformation

$$
x _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ n e w } } ^ { 0 } = x _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ o l d } } ^ { 0 } - \frac 1 2 \ : \boldsymbol { \cal { \Sigma } } \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot \nu ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ] } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } \ : d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime }
$$

Rigidity of coordinate system

(see exercise 39.9). Thus, there is no necessity to include the $\pmb { \Sigma }$ term.

The absence of the $\pmb { \Sigma }$ term from $g _ { 0 0 }$ means that the time coordinate has been fixed rigidly up through post-Newtonian order:

The space coordinates are also fixed rigidly through post-Newtonian order:

because any transformation of the form

$$
x _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ n e w } } ^ { j } = x _ { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ o l d } } ^ { j } + \mathrm { \ p o s i t i o n - d e p e n d e n t { \mathrm { ~ t e r m s ~ o f ~ } } } O ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } R _ { \odot } )
$$

# Summary of PPN metric and parameters

would destroy the form (39.23b) of the space part of the metric.

In summary, for almost every metric theory of gravity yet invented, accurate through post-Newtonian order the metric coefficients have the form (39.23). One theory is distinguished from another by the values of its ten “post-Newtonian parameters" $\beta , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 3 } , \beta _ { 4 } , \gamma , \zeta , \eta , \varDelta _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { 4 _ { 2 } }$ .These are determined by comparing the field equations of the given theory with the form (39.23) of the post-Newtonian metric. The parameter values for general relativity and for several other theories are given in Box 39.2,along with a heuristic description of each parameter.

# EXERCISES

# ExerCise 39.8. ABSENCE OF "METRIC-GENERATES-METRIC" TERMS IN POST-NEWTONIAN LIMIT

In writing down the post-Newtonian metric corrections,one might be tempted to include terms that are generated by the Newtonian potential acting alone,without any direct aid from the matter. After all, general relativity and other metric theories are nonlinear; so the two-step process (matter) $ U $ (post-Newtonian metric corrections） seems quite natural. Show that such terms are not needed, because the equations (39.l4)-(39.l6) of the Newtonian approximation enable one to reexpress them in terms of direct integrals over the matter distribution. In particular, show that

$$
\int \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) / \partial { x _ { j } } ^ { \prime } \partial t } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } = 2 \pi [ V _ { j } ( x , t ) - W _ { j } ( x , t ) ]
$$

where $V _ { \mathbf { j } }$ and $W _ { j }$ are defined by equations (39.23c,d); also show that

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \int \frac { [ \partial U ( \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) / \partial \mathbf { x } _ { j } ^ { \prime } ] [ \partial U ( \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) / \partial x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ] } { | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad = - 2 \pi [ U ( \mathbf { x } , t ) ] ^ { 2 } + 4 \pi \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) U ( \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | \mathbf { x } - \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } \mathbf { x } ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Note that the terms on the righthand sides of (39.27) and (39.28) are already included in the expressions $_ { ( 3 9 . 2 3 e , f ) }$ for ${ \pmb g _ { 0 j } }$ and $g _ { 0 0 }$

# \$39.9. VELOCITY OF PPN COORDINATES RELATIVE TO "UNIVERSAL REST FRAME"

Thus far it has been assumed tacitly that the center of mass of the solar system is at rest in the PPN coordinate system. Is this really a permissible assumption? Put differently,can one always so adjust the PPN coordinate system that its origin moves with any desired velocity (e.g., that of the solar system); or is the PPN coordinate system rigidly and irrevocably attached to some “universal rest frame"?

In general relativity, the geometry of curved spacetime picks out no preferred coordinate frames (except in cases with special symmetry). Therefore, one expects the velocity of the PPN coordinate frame to be freely specifiable. Put differently, one expects the entire PPN formalism, for general relativity,to be invariant under Lorentz transformations of the PPN coordinates [combined, perhaps,with “infinitesimal coordinate transformations” to maintain the gauge conditions that the $\mathbf { \mathfrak { s } } \mathbf { \underline { { { \mathbf { v } } } } } \mathbf { \mathfrak { s } }$ and $^ { \bullet \bullet } U _ { j k } ^ { \quad \bullet \bullet }$ terms of (39.24) and (39.23a) be absent]. By contrast, in Ni's theory of gravity (Box 39.1) the geometry of spacetime always picks out a preferred coordinate frame: the “rest frame of the universe.” One would not be surprised, in this case, to find the PPN coordinate frame rigidly attached to the universal rest frame.

The above intuition is correct, according to calculations by Will (1971d) and by Will and Nordtvedt (l972). When dealing with general relativity and other theories with little or no“prior geometry,” one can freely specify the velocity of the PPN coordinate system (at some initial instant of time). But for theories like Ni's,with a preferred “universal rest frame"("preferred-frame theories"),only in the preferred frame can the post-Newtonian metric assume the form derived in the last section [equations (39.23)]. This restriction on the PPN metric does not mean that one is confined, in preferred-frame theories,to perform all calculations in the universal rest frame. Rather, it means that for such theories the PPN metric requires generalization to take account of coordinate-frame motion relative to the universal rest frame.

The required generalization can be achieved by subjecting the PPN metric (39.23) to (l)a Lorentz boost from the preferred frame $\{ x _ { \tt O L D } ^ { \alpha } \}$ to a new PPN frame $\{ x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ^ { \alpha } \}$ ， which moves with velocity $\pmb { \nu }$ , plus (2) a change of gauge designed to keep the metric coefficients as simple as possible. The boost-plus-gauge-change is [Will and Nordtvedt (1972))

# Preferred-frame theories of gravity

Generalization of PPN metric to moving frames

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { O L D } } } = x _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } \cdot w ) w + \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } w ^ { 2 } \right) w t _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } } \\ & { ~ + ~ \displaystyle O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } t _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } + \epsilon ^ { 4 } x _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } ) , } \\ & { t _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { O L D } } } = t _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } w ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } w ^ { 4 } \right) + \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } w ^ { 2 } \right) x _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \mathrm { N E W } } } \cdot w } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \chi ( t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } , x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ) \equiv - \int \rho _ { o } ( t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } , x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ^ { \prime } ) | x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } - x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ^ { \prime } | d ^ { 3 } x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

[Note: One insists,in the spirit of the post-Newtonian approximation, that the velocity $\pmb { \nu }$ of the new PPN frame relative to the universal rest frame be no larger than the characteristic internal velocities of the system:

$$
| w | \lesssim \epsilon . ]
$$

This change of coordinates produces corresponding changes in the velocity of the matter

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { O L D } } = \frac { d x _ { \mathrm { O L D } } } { d t _ { \mathrm { O L D } } } = \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { N E W } } \bigg ( 1 - w \cdot \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { N E W } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } w ^ { 2 } \bigg ) } \\ { \displaystyle + ~ w \bigg ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } w \cdot \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { N E W } } \bigg ) + ~ O ( \mathfrak { c } ^ { 5 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

A long but straightforward calculation (exercise 39.11) yields the following components for the metric in the new PPN coordinates. [Note: The subscripts NEW are here and hereafter dropped from the notation.]

Final form of metric

$$
g _ { j k } = \delta _ { j k } ( 1 + 2 \gamma U ) + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) ,
$$

$$
g _ { 0 j } = - \frac { 7 } { 2 } { \cal { A } } _ { 1 } V _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \cal { A } } _ { 2 } W _ { j } + \left( \alpha _ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 } \right) w _ { j } U - \alpha _ { 2 } w _ { k } U _ { k j } + { \cal { O } } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r } { g _ { 0 0 } = - 1 + 2 U - 2 \beta U ^ { 2 } + 4 \Psi - \zeta \mathcal { Q } - \eta \mathcal { P } } \\ { + ( \alpha _ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 3 } - \alpha _ { 1 } ) w ^ { 2 } U + ( 2 \alpha _ { 3 } - \alpha _ { 1 } ) w _ { j } V _ { j } - \alpha _ { 2 } w _ { j } w _ { k } U _ { j k } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 6 } ) . } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

Here ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 2 } ,$ and ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 3 }$ are certain combinations of PPN parameters

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \alpha _ { 1 } = 7 \mathrm { ~ } \varDelta _ { 1 } + \mathrm { ~ } \varDelta _ { 2 } - 4 \gamma - 4 , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \alpha _ { 2 } = \varDelta _ { 2 } + \zeta - 1 , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \alpha _ { 3 } = 4 \beta _ { 1 } - 2 \gamma - 2 - \zeta . } } \end{array}
$$

The“gravitational potentials” $\mathcal { I } , V _ { j } , W _ { j } , \Psi , a ,$ and $\boldsymbol { \phi }$ appearing here are to be calculated in the new,“moving” PPN coordinate system by the same prescriptions

as one used in the universal rest frame.Thus, their functional forms are the same as previously,but their values at any given event are different (see exercise 39.11):

$$
U ( \mathbf { x } , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | \mathbf { x } - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ;
$$

$$
V _ { j } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) v _ { j } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ;
$$

$$
W _ { j } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot v ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ] ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } ;
$$

$$
\Psi ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) \psi ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
\psi = \beta _ { 1 } v ^ { 2 } + \beta _ { 2 } U + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta _ { 3 } \varPi + { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \beta _ { 4 } p / \rho _ { o } ;
$$

$$
\mathcal { Q } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) [ ( x - x ^ { \prime } ) \cdot v ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) ] ^ { 2 } } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } ;
$$

$$
\mathcal { D } ( x , t ) = \int \frac { \bigg [ t _ { \uparrow \hat { k } } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \delta _ { j k } t _ { l l } ( x ^ { \prime } , t ) \bigg ] ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) ( x _ { k } - x _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) } { | x - x ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } .
$$

The quantity $U _ { j k }$ is the gravitational potential defined in equation (39.23a):

$$
U _ { j k } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , t ) = \int \frac { \rho _ { o } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime } , t ) ( x _ { j } - x _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) ( x _ { k } - x _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) } { | { \boldsymbol { x } } - { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime } | ^ { 3 } } d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } .
$$

Notice that the velocity $\pmb { w }$ of the PPN coordinate system relative to the universal rest frame appears explicitly in the PPN metric only if one or more of the coefficients $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \alpha _ { 3 } ,$ is nonzero. Thus, theories with $\alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha _ { 3 } = 0$ (e.g., general relativity) possess no preferred universal rest frame in the post-Newtonian limit; all their PPN frames are“created equal.” By contrast, theories with at least one of $\alpha _ { \bf 1 } , \alpha _ { \bf 2 } , \alpha _ { \bf 3 } ,$ nonzero (e.g., Ni's theory) do possess a preferred frame.

The generalized form (39.32)of the PPN metric,by virtue of the process used to construct it, is invariant under a Lorentz boost plus a gauge adjustment ["Post-Galilean transformation”; see Chandrasekhar and Contopolous (1967)):

Post-Galilean invariance

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle x _ { \mathrm { o L D } } = x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } + \frac 1 2 ( x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } \cdot \beta ) \beta + \left( 1 + \frac 1 2 \beta ^ { 2 } \right) \dot { \beta } t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \left. O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } + \epsilon ^ { 4 } x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ) , \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle t _ { \mathrm { O L D } } = \left( 1 + \frac 1 2 \beta ^ { 2 } + \frac 3 8 \beta ^ { 4 } \right) t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } + \left( 1 + \frac 1 2 \beta ^ { 2 } \right) x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } \cdot \beta } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \left( \frac 1 2 A _ { 2 } + \xi _ { 1 } - 1 \right) \beta \cdot \nabla _ { \mathrm { N E W } } \chi + O ( \epsilon ^ { 6 } t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } + \epsilon ^ { 5 } x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Of course, it is also invariant under spatial rotations.

# \$39.10. PPN STRESS-ENERGY TENSOR

Themotioofasd $T ^ { \alpha \beta } { } _ { ; \beta } = 0$ Before studying them, one must calculate the post-Newtonian corrections to the stres-energy tensor in the PPN coordinate frame.This requires a transformation from the comoving,orthonormal frame $\pmb { \omega } ^ { \hat { \pmb { \alpha } } }$ ,where

$$
T ^ { \hat { 0 } \hat { 0 } } = \rho _ { o } ( 1 + \varPi ) , T ^ { \hat { 0 } \hat { j } } = 0 , T ^ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } = t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } ,
$$

to the coordinate frame. One can effect this transformation in two stages: stage 2 is a transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \omega ^ { \tilde { 0 } } \equiv \left[ 1 - U + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) \right] d t } & { } & { ( 3 9 . } \\ { + \left[ \frac { 7 } { 2 } A _ { 1 } V _ { j } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } A _ { 2 } W _ { j } + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha _ { 2 } \right) w _ { j } U + \alpha _ { 2 } w _ { k } U _ { k j } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) \right] d x ^ { j } , } \\ { \omega ^ { \tilde { j } } \equiv \left[ \left( 1 + \gamma U \right) \delta _ { j k } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) \right] d x ^ { k } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) d t , } & { } & { ( 3 9 . } \end{array}
$$

between the coordinate frame and an orthonormal frame attached to it; stage l is a pure Lorentz transformation (boost) between the two orthonormal frames $\pmb { \omega } ^ { \widetilde { \alpha } }$ and $\omega ^ { \hat { \alpha } }$ .The 4-velocity of this boost is minus the 4-velocity of the matter,which has components

$$
u ^ { j } = v _ { j } u ^ { 0 } , \qquad u ^ { 0 } = 1 + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } v ^ { 2 } + U + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) \qquad \mathrm { i n ~ c o o r d . ~ f r a m e } ;
$$

$$
\left. \begin{array} { l } { { u ^ { \tilde { j } } = v _ { \tilde { j } } u ^ { \tilde { 0 } } , \qquad u ^ { \tilde { 0 } } = 1 + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) , \Biggl \vert ~ \mathrm { i n } ~ \pmb { \omega } ^ { \tilde { \alpha } } \mathrm { ~ f r a m e } . } } \\ { { v _ { \tilde { j } } = v _ { j } [ 1 + ( 1 + \gamma ) U ] } } \end{array} \right.
$$

Combining the boost,which has ordinary velocity $\beta _ { \widetilde { j } } = - v _ { \widetilde { j } } ,$ with the transformation (39.38),and then inverting, one obtains the result (exercise 39.12)

Transformation from rest frame of matter to PPN coordinate frame

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { d ^ { 0 } { } _ { 6 } = 1 + \displaystyle \frac 1 2 v ^ { 2 } + U + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { d ^ { 0 } { } _ { j } = v _ { j } \displaystyle \left[ 1 + \displaystyle \frac 1 2 v ^ { 2 } + ( 2 + \gamma ) U \right] - \displaystyle \frac 7 2 A _ { 1 } V _ { j } - \displaystyle \frac 1 2 A _ { 2 } W _ { j } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad + \left( \alpha _ { 2 } - \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \right) w _ { j } U - \alpha _ { 2 } v _ { k } U _ { k j } + { \ \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { d ^ { i } { } _ { 0 } = v _ { j } \displaystyle \left[ 1 + \displaystyle \frac 1 2 v ^ { 2 } + U \right] + { \ \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { d ^ { i } { } _ { k } = ( 1 - \gamma U ) \delta _ { j k } + \displaystyle \frac 1 2 v _ { j } v _ { k } + { \ \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

6

This transformation, when applied to the stressenergy tensor (39.37) yields, in the PPN coordinate frame,

$$

# \$39.11. PPN EQUATIONS OF MOTION

The post-Newtonian corrections to the Newtonian equations of motion (39.15) and (39.16) are derived from the law of conservation of baryon number $\big ( \rho _ { o } u ^ { \alpha } \big ) _ { ; \alpha } = 0$ ， andfromthelawofconservationoflocalenergymomentum, $T ^ { \alpha \beta } { } _ { ; \beta } = 0$ The simplest of the equations of motion is the conservation of baryon number. Its exact expression is ${ \bar { ( \rho _ { o } u ^ { \alpha } ) } } _ { ; \alpha } = ( 1 / { \sqrt { - g } } ) ( { \sqrt { - g } } \rho _ { o } u ^ { \alpha } ) _ { , \alpha } = 0 .$ Define a new quantity

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho ^ { * } \equiv \rho _ { o } \Big ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } v ^ { 2 } + 3 \gamma U \Big ) } \\ { = \rho _ { o } u ^ { 0 } \sqrt { - g } + O ( \rho _ { o } \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) } \end{array}
$$

[see (39.39) for $u ^ { 0 }$ ，and (39.32） for the metric]. Then rest-mass conservation takes on the same form as at the Newtonian order (39.15a), except now it is more accurate:

$$
\rho ^ { \ast } , _ { t } + ( \rho ^ { \ast } v _ { j } ) _ { , j } = 0 + \mathrm { e r r o r s ~ o f ~ } O ( \rho _ { o , j } \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) \mathrm { . }
$$

The next simplest equationof motion is $T ^ { 0 \alpha } { } _ { ; \alpha } = 0$ . Straightforward evaluation, using the metric of equations (39.32)and the stress-energy tensor of equations (9.42), yields

$$
\begin{array} { r } { [ \rho _ { o } ( 1 + \Pi + v ^ { 2 } + 2 U ) ] _ { , t } + [ \rho _ { o } ( 1 + \Pi + v ^ { 2 } + 2 U ) v _ { j } + t _ { \hat { j } \hat { m } } v _ { m } ] _ { , j } \qquad } \\ { + ( 3 \gamma - 2 ) \rho _ { o } U _ { , t } + ( 3 \gamma - 3 ) \rho _ { o } v _ { k } U _ { , k } = O ( \rho _ { o , j } \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

By subtracting equation (39.44) from this,and using the Newtonian equations of motion (39.15) and (39.16) to simplify several terms where the Newtonian approximation is adequate, one obtains

Law of energy conservation

$$
\rho _ { o } d { \Pi } / d t + t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } v _ { { j } , k } = 0 + \mathrm { e r r o r s ~ o f } \ O ( \rho _ { o , j } \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) .
$$

Notice that this is nothing but the first law of thermodynamics (local energy conservation) with energy flow through the matter being neglected. (Neglecting energy flow was justified in $\ S 3 9 . 5 .$ ）This first law of thermodynamics is actually a post-Newtonian equation in the context of hydrodynamics,rather than a Newtonian equation, because $\pi$ does not affect the hydrodynamic motion at Newtonian order (see $\ S 3 9 . 7 )$

The last of the equationsof motion, $T ^ { j \alpha } { } _ { ; \alpha } = 0$ ，reduces to the post-Newtonian Euler equation

Post-Newtonian Euler equation

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \rho ^ { \alpha } \frac { d u _ { j } } { d t } - \rho ^ { \alpha } U _ { j , j } + [ t _ { j k } ( 1 + 3 \gamma U ) ] _ { , k } - t _ { j k , k } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathbf { \nu } _ { j k } ^ { 2 } + I I \right) - \frac { t _ { j k } t _ { j k , l } } { \rho ^ { \alpha } } } \\ & { \qquad + \rho ^ { \ast } \frac { d } { d t } \Big [ ( 2 \gamma + 2 ) U \upsilon _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \mathcal { T } _ { \mathcal { A } _ { 1 } } + \mathcal { A } _ { 2 } ) V _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 } U w _ { j } \Big ] - \upsilon _ { j } \rho ^ { \alpha } U _ { , i } + \upsilon _ { k } t _ { j k , i } } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 1 } { 2 } A _ { 2 } \rho ^ { \alpha } ( V _ { j } - W _ { j } ) _ { , i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \rho ^ { \ast } [ ( \mathcal { A } _ { 1 } + \mathcal { A } _ { 2 } ) \upsilon _ { k } + ( \alpha _ { 1 } - 2 \alpha _ { 3 } ) \omega _ { k } ] V _ { k , j } } \\ & { \qquad - \rho ^ { \ast } \Big [ 2 \Psi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \xi d - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \eta \vartheta - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } \upsilon _ { i } \nu _ { k } U _ { k + } + \alpha _ { 2 } \upsilon _ { i } ( V _ { i } - W _ { i } ) \Big ] _ { , j } } \\ & { \qquad - \rho ^ { \ast } U _ { j } \Big [ \gamma ^ { \alpha } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 } \pm \nu _ { 1 } \cdot \mathbf { \nu } _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \alpha _ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 3 } - \alpha _ { 1 } ) w ^ { 2 } - ( 2 \beta - 2 ) U + \frac { 3 \gamma \beta / \rho ^ { \alpha } } { \gamma ^ { 2 } } \Big ] } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \tau _ { j k } t _ { j k } \alpha _ { m } - t _ { j m } \upsilon _ { m k } \varepsilon _ { k } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \upsilon _ { m } ( t _ { j m } \nu _ { k } ) _ { , k } - \upsilon _ { i } ( t _ { k l } \nu _ { k } ) _ { , l } ] = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Partial derivatives are denoted by commas; $d / d t$ is the time-derivative following the matter [equation (39.16)].

Equations (39.44), (39.46),and (39.47) are a complete set of equations:of motion at the post-Newtonian order.

$$

$$

Here square brackets on tensor indices denote antisymmetrization, and round brackets denote symmetrization. As part of the derivation, it may be useful to prove and use the relations

$$
\begin{array} { c l } { { } } & { { \displaystyle x ( t , x ) = - \int \rho _ { o } ( t , x ^ { \prime } ) | x - x ^ { \prime } | ~ d ^ { 3 } x ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \chi _ { , j k } = - \delta _ { j k } U + U _ { j k } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle - \left. \begin{array} { l } { { } } \\ { { \chi _ { , i t } = V _ { i } - W _ { i } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) , } } \end{array} \right. } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { W _ { [ k , j ] } = V _ { [ k , j ] } . } } \end{array}
$$

Here $x$ is the function originally defined in equation (39.29c).

ExerCise 39.14. POST-NEWTONIAN APPROXIMATION TO GENERAL RELATIVITY Perform a post-Newtonian expansion of Einstein's field equations,thereby obtaining the values cited in Box 39.2 for the PPN parameters of general relativity. The calculations might best follow the approach of Chandrasekhar (l965a): Set $g _ { \alpha \beta } = \eta _ { \alpha \beta } + h _ { \alpha \beta }$ and assume

$$
h _ { 0 0 } = { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) , \qquad h _ { 0 j } = { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 3 } ) , \qquad h _ { j k } = { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Choose the space and time coordinates so that the four “gauge conditions"

$$
\left. \begin{array} { l } { { h _ { j k , k } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } h _ { , j } = \cal O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } / R _ { \odot } ) } } \\ { { } } \\ { { h _ { 0 k , k } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } h _ { k k , 0 } = \cal O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } / R _ { \odot } ) } } \end{array} \right\} \mathrm { w i t h } h = h _ { \alpha \beta } \eta ^ { \alpha \beta } = - h _ { 0 0 } + h _ { \iota t }
$$

are satisfied.

(a) Show that the spatial gauge conditions are the post-Newtonian approximations to those (35.la) used in the study of weak gravitational waves,but that the temporal gauge condition is not.

(b) Use these gauge conditions and the post-Newtonian limit in equations (8.24) and (8.47) to obtain for the Ricci tensor,accurate to linearized order,

$$
R _ { 0 0 } = { - \frac 1 2 } h _ { 0 0 , m \dot { m } } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } / R _ { \odot } { } ^ { 2 } ) , R _ { j k } = - \frac 1 2 h _ { j \dot { k } , m m } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } / R _ { \odot } { } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
R _ { 0 j } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } h _ { 0 j , m m } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } h _ { 0 0 , 0 j } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } / { R _ { \odot } } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

(c) Combine these with the Newtonian form (39.13) of the stress-energy tensor,and with equation (39.27), to obtain the following metric coefficients, accurate to linearized order:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { h _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } = 2 U + k _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 6 } ) , \qquad h _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 j } = - \frac { 7 } { 2 } V _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } W _ { j } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) , } } \\ & { } & { \stackrel { \mathrm { ! } } { \longrightarrow } [ { \mathrm { u n k n o w n ~ p o s t } } \cdot { \mathrm { N e w t o n i a n ~ c o r r e c t i o n } } ] } \end{array}
$$

$$
h _ { j k } = 2 U \delta _ { j k } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) .
$$

Here $U , V _ { j } ,$ and $W _ { j }$ are to be regarded as defined by equations (39.34a,b,c). By comparing these metric coeficients with equations (39.32),discover that

$$
\gamma = 1 , \qquad A _ { 1 } = 1 , \qquad A _ { 2 } = 1
$$

for general relativity.

(d) With this knowledge of the metric in linearized order,one can carry out the analysis of $\ S 3 9 . 1 0$ (using $\gamma = \varDelta _ { 1 } = \varDelta _ { 2 } = 1$ throughout), to obtain the post-Newtonian corrections to the stress-energy tensor [equation (39.42) with $\gamma = 1 ]$

(e)Calculate,arlytepostNewtonacorrections totheicitensot $R _ { 0 0 } ,$ using $g _ { \alpha \beta } = \eta _ { \alpha \beta } + h _ { \alpha \beta }$ using $h _ { \alpha \beta }$ as given in equations (39.54),and using the gauge conditions (39.52).The answer should be

$$
R _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } = \bigg ( - U - \frac { 1 } { 2 } k _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } - U ^ { 2 } \bigg ) _ { , m m } + 4 U U _ { , m m } + O ( \epsilon ^ { 6 } / { R _ { \odot } } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

(f) Evaluate the Einstein equation $R _ { 0 0 } = 8 \pi ( T _ { 0 0 } - \sharp g _ { 0 0 } T )$ ， accurate to post-Newtonian order,and solve it to obtain the post-Newtonian metric correction

$$
k _ { 0 0 } = - 2 U ^ { 2 } + 4 \Psi ,
$$

where $\Psi$ is given by equation (39.43d) with $\beta _ { 1 } = \beta _ { 2 } = \beta _ { 3 } = \beta _ { 4 } = 1$ 、By comparing with equations (39.32c) and (39.34d), discover that

$$
\beta = \beta _ { 1 } = \beta _ { 2 } = \beta _ { 3 } = \beta _ { 4 } = 1 , ~ \xi = \eta = 0
$$

for general relativity.

(g) Knowing the full post-Newtonian metric, and the full post-Newtonian stress-energy tensor,one can carry out the calculations of $\ S 3 9 . 1 1$ (using $\gamma = \beta = \beta _ { 1 } = \beta _ { 2 } = \beta _ { 3 } = \beta _ { 4 } =$ $\varDelta _ { 1 } = \varDelta _ { 2 } = 1$ ， $\zeta = \eta = 0$ ）to obtain the post-Newtonian equations of motion for the matter [equations (39.44), (39.46),and (39.47)].

# $\$ 39.12$ . RELATION OF PPN COORDINATES TOSURROUNDING UNIVERSE

One crucial issue remains to be clarified: What is the orientation of the PPN coordinate system relative to the surrounding universe? More particularly: Does the PPN coordinate system rotate relative to the“fixed stars on the sky;” or is it “rigidly attached” to them,in some sense? In order to answer this question, imagine using the PPN formalism to analyze the solar system.Make no assumptions about the solar system's velocity through the PPN coordinate frame. Then,as one moves outward from the Sun, past the Earth's orbit, past Pluto's orbit, and on out toward interstellar space, one sees the PPN coordinate frame become more and more Lorentz in its global properties $[ g _ { \alpha \beta } = \eta _ { \alpha \beta } + O ( M _ { \odot } / r ) ]$ Thus,far from the solar system the PPN coordinates become a “Lorentz frame moving through the galaxy.” This means, of course, that the spatial axes of the PPN coordinate frame behave as though they were attached to gyroscopes far outside the solar system. Equivalently: The PPN coordinate system Fermi-Walker-transports its spatial axes through the spacetime geometry of the galaxy and universe.

Solar system's PPN coordinate frame is attached to a local Lorentz frame of Galaxy

# \$39.13. SUMMARY OF PPN FORMALISM

#

The PPN formalism,as constructed in this chapter, is summarized in Box 39.4. Much of the recent literature uses a different set of PPN parameters than are used in this book;for a translation from one parameter set to the other, see Box 39.5.

# Exercise 39.15. MANY-BODY SYSTEM IN POST-NEWTONIAN LIMIT OF GENERAL RELATIVITY

Consider,in the post-Newtonian limit of general relativity,a system made up of many gravitationally interacting bodies with separations large compared to their sizes (example: the solar system). Idealize each body to be spherically symmetric, to be free of internal motions,and to have isotropic internal stresses, $t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } = \delta _ { j k } p$ .Let the world line of the center of body $\pmb { A }$ ,in some chosen PPN coordinate frame,be ${ \pmb x } _ { A } ( t )$ ； and let the (coordinate) velocity of the center of body $\pmb { A }$ be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \nu _ { A } ( t ) = d x _ { A } / d t . } \end{array}
$$

The total mass-energy of body $\pmb { A }$ as measured in its neighborhood (rest mass-energy plus internal energy plus self-gravitational energy) is given by

$$
M _ { A } = \int _ { \mathcal { V } _ { A } } \Big ( 1 + \varPi - \frac { 1 } { 2 } U _ { \mathrm { s e l f } } \Big ) d ( \mathrm { r e s t \ m a s s } ) + O ( M _ { A } \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) ,
$$

where $U _ { \mathrm { s e l f } }$ is the body's own Newtonian potential (no contributions from other bodies), and $\tilde { \nu } _ { A }$ is the interior of the body.

(continued on page 1094)

# Box 39.4 SUMMARY OF THE PPN FORMALISM

I. Variables

$\rho _ { o } ( x , t )$ : baryon “mass” density (S39.3),as measured in rest frame $\pi ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , t )$ : specific internal energy (dimensionless; $\ S 3 9 . 3 )$ ,as measured in rest frame $t _ { \hat { j } \hat { k } } ( x , t )$ : components of stress referred to orthonormal axes of rest frame $v _ { j } ( x , t )$ : coordinate velocity of matter (i.e., rest frame) relative to PPN coordinates $U ( { \pmb x } , t ) , \Psi ( { \pmb x } , t ) , \mathcal { I } ( { \pmb x } , t ) , \mathcal { I } ( { \pmb x } , t ) ,$ $V _ { j } ( x , t ) , W _ { j } ( x , t ) .$ $U _ { j k } ( \pmb { x } , t )$ gravitational potentials ${ \gamma , \beta , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 3 } , \beta _ { 4 } , \varDelta _ { 1 } , \varDelta _ { 2 } , \xi , \eta } .$ parameters whose values distinguish one theory from another (see Box 39.2)

$\pmb { w }$ :velocity of PPN coordinate frame relative to “universal rest frame”[relevant only for theories with nonzero ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \mathbf { 1 } } , { \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \mathbf { 2 } }$ or ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 3 }$ ； see eq. (39.33)].

II. Equations governing evolution of these variables $\pmb { \rho } _ { o }$ : conservation of rest mass, equation (39.44)

$\pi$ : first law of thermodynamics, equation (39.46)

$t _ { \widehat { j } \widehat { k } }$ : determined in terms of $\pmb { \rho } _ { o } , \pi$ ,and other material variables (chemical composition, strains, etc.) by equations of state and the usual theory of a stressed medium-which is not discussed here

$v _ { j }$ : equations of motion ( $" ^ { 6 6 } F = m \bullet " .$ ), equations (39.47) $U , \Psi , a , \mathcal { D } , V _ { j } , W _ { j } , U _ { j k }$ : source equations (39.34)

III. Quantities to be calculated from these variables

$g _ { 0 0 } ( x , t ) , g _ { 0 j } ( x , t ) , g _ { j k } ( x , t )$ ： these components of metric in PPN coordinate frame are expressed in terms of gravitational potentials by equations (39.32)

$u ^ { 0 } ( x , t ) , u ^ { j } ( x , t )$ : these components of matter 4-velocity in PPN coordinate frame are given by equations (39.39)

$T ^ { 0 0 } ( x , t ) , \ T ^ { 0 j } ( x , t ) , \ T ^ { j k } ( x , t )$ : these components of stress-energy tensor in PPN coordinate frame are given by equations (39.42)

IV. Relation between rest frame, PPN coordinates,and the universe

1. Orthonormal basis $\omega ^ { \hat { \alpha } }$ of rest frame, where $t _ { \widehat { j } \widehat { k } }$ are defined, is related to PPN coordinate basis $\pmb { d x ^ { \alpha } }$ by equations (39.41)

2. Far from the sun, the PPN coordinates become asymptotically Lorentz; i.e, they form an inertial frame moving through the spacetime geometry of the galaxy and the universe.

3. Gives no account of expansion of universe or of cosmic gravitational waves impinging on solar system.

# Box 39.5 PPN PARAMETERS USED IN LITERATURE: A TRANSLATOR'S GUIDE

The original “point-particle version" of the PPN formalism [Nordtvedt (l968b)],and the original “perfect-fluid version"[Will (l97lc)] used different sets of PPN parameters.This book has adopted Will's set,and has added the parameter $\eta$ characterizing effects of anisotropic stresses.More recently, Wil and Nordtvedt have jointly adopted a revised set of parameters,described below.

# B. Significance of Revised Parameters

${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 2 } , { \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 3 }$ measure the extent of and nature of “preferred-frame effects"; see $\ S 3 9 . 9$ Any theory of gravity with at least one $\pmb { \alpha }$ nonzero is called a prefered-frame theory. $\boldsymbol { \zeta } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { \zeta } _ { 2 } , \boldsymbol { \zeta } _ { 3 } , \boldsymbol { \zeta } _ { 4 } , \alpha _ { 3 }$ measure the extent of and nature of breakdowns in global conservation laws. A theory of gravity possesses,at the post-Newtonian level,all I0 global conservation laws (4 for energy-momentum,6 for angular momentum; see Chapters 19 and 20) if and only if $\zeta _ { 1 } = \zeta _ { 2 } = \zeta _ { 3 } = \zeta _ { 4 } = \alpha _ { 3 } = 0$ .See Will (1971d), Will and Nordtvedt (1972),Will (1972), for proofs and discussion. Any theory with ${ \boldsymbol { \zeta } } _ { 1 } = { \boldsymbol { \zeta } } _ { 2 }$ $= \xi _ { 3 } = \xi _ { 4 } = \alpha _ { 3 } = 0$ is called a conservative theory.

In general relativity and the Dicke-Brans-Jordan theory, all α's and \$'s vanish. Thus,general relativity and Dicke-Brans-Jordan are conservative theories with no preferred-frame effects.

(a) Show that, when written in the chosen PPN coordinate frame, this expression for $M _ { A }$ becomes

$$
M _ { A } = \int _ { \mathcal { V } _ { A } } \rho _ { o } \left( 1 + \varPi _ { + } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \nu _ { A } } ^ { 2 } + 3 U - \frac { 1 } { 2 } U _ { s \mathrm { e l f } } \right) d ^ { 3 } x + O ( M _ { A } \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) .
$$

Use equations (39.43),(39.44),and (39.46) to show that $M _ { A }$ is conserved as the bodies move about, $d M _ { A } / d t = 0$

(b） Pick an event $( t , \pmb { x } )$ outside all the bodies,and at time $t$ denote

r _ { A } \equiv x _ { A } - x , r _ { A B } \equiv x _ { A } - x _ { B } , r _ { A } \equiv | r _ { A } | , r _ { A B } \equiv | r _ { A B } | .
$$

$$

where the integral is performed in the PPN frame.]

(c) Perform an infinitesimal coordinate transformation,

$$
t _ { \mathsf { O L D } } = t _ { \mathrm { N E W } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } ( \boldsymbol { r } _ { A } \cdot \boldsymbol { \nu } _ { A } ) } { \boldsymbol { r } _ { A } } , \qquad x _ { \mathrm { O L D } } = x _ { \mathrm { N E W } } ,
$$

to bring the metric (39.60) into the standard form originally devised by Einstein,Infeld,and Hoffman (l938),and by Eddington and Clark (1938):

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle g _ { \hbar } = \delta _ { \hbar } \left( 1 + 2 \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } } { r _ { A } } \right) + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 4 } ) } , }  \\ { { \displaystyle g _ { 0 j } = - 4 \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } } { r _ { A } } v _ { A j } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 5 } ) } , }  \\ { { \displaystyle g _ { 0 0 } = - 1 + 2 \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } } { r _ { A } } - 2 \left( \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } } { r _ { A } } \right) ^ { 2 } + 3 \sum _ { A } \frac { M _ { A } v _ { A } ^ { 2 } } { r _ { A } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - 2 \sum _ { A } \sum _ { B \neq A } \frac { M _ { A } M _ { B } } { r _ { A } r _ { B B } } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \chi } { \partial t ^ { 2 } } + { \cal O } ( \epsilon ^ { 6 } ) } , }  \end{array}
$$

where $x$ [equation (39.49a)] is given by
